section.title: God's Righteous Judgment
section.sectionRationale: Having exposed the open rebellion of the pagan world in chapter 1, Paul now turns and points his finger at the respectable moralist who nods along in agreement. The very act of condemning others convicts the judge, for he does the same things. God's judgment is impartial and according to truth: He will repay each one according to his deeds, whether Jew or Greek. Even Gentiles who never had the written law show its work written on their hearts, so that no one is left with an excuse.
section.thesis: God judges every person righteously, impartially, and according to what they have actually done; therefore the moralist who condemns others while practicing the same sins is not exempt but doubly guilty, and the whole human race, with the law written either on stone or on the heart, stands accountable before Him.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the self-condemning judge who has no excuse; God repaying each according to his deeds without favoritism; and the coming day when God judges the secrets of all, Jew and Gentile alike, doers rather than hearers being declared righteous.

units.label: No excuse for the one who judges
units.note: the judge condemned by his own verdict

verses.text: You, therefore, have no excuse, you who {{krino|pass judgment}} on another. For on whatever grounds you judge the other, you are condemning yourself, because you who pass judgment do the same things.
And we know that God's {{krima|judgment}} against those who do such things is based on truth.
So when you, O man, pass judgment on others, yet do the same things, do you think you will escape God's judgment?
Or do you disregard the riches of His kindness, tolerance, and patience, not realizing that God's kindness leads you to {{metanoia|repentance}}?
But because of your hard and unrepentant heart, you are storing up wrath against yourself for the day of wrath, when God's {{dikaiokrisia|righteous judgment}} will be revealed.

units.label: God will repay each according to his deeds
units.note: impartial judgment, first for the Jew, then for the Greek

verses.text: God “will repay each one according to his deeds.”
To those who by perseverance in doing good seek glory, honor, and immortality, He will give eternal life.
But for those who are self-seeking and who reject the truth and follow wickedness, there will be wrath and anger.
There will be trouble and distress for every human being who does evil, first for the Jew, then for the Greek;
but glory, honor, and peace for everyone who does good, first for the Jew, then for the Greek.
For God does not show favoritism.

units.label: Doers, not hearers; the law written on the heart
units.note: the day God judges the secrets of all through Christ

verses.text: All who sin apart from the law will also perish apart from the law, and all who sin under the law will be judged by the law.
For it is not the hearers of the law who are righteous before God, but it is the doers of the law who will be declared righteous.
Indeed, when Gentiles, who do not have the law, do by nature what the law requires, they are a law to themselves, even though they do not have the law.
So they show that the work of the law is written on their hearts, their {{syneidesis|consciences}} also bearing witness, and their thoughts either accusing or defending them
on the day when God will judge men's secrets through Christ Jesus, as proclaimed by my gospel.

krino.gloss: To judge, to pass sentence, to condemn. Paul uses the verb against the moralist who sets himself up as a judge over others. The irony is that by his own standard he pronounces his own guilt, for he does the very things he condemns. To judge another is to publish the law by which you yourself will be measured.

krima.gloss: The verdict or sentence, the result of judging. God's krima is grounded in truth, not appearance; He sees what is really done and pronounces accordingly. Unlike human judgment, which is partial and often hypocritical, God's sentence is perfectly accurate and cannot be escaped.

metanoia.gloss: A change of mind that turns the whole person around, a turning from sin to God. God's kindness, tolerance, and patience are not indifference to sin but a summons; they are meant to lead the sinner to repentance. To despise that kindness by presuming on it is to store up wrath instead.

dikaiokrisia.gloss: Righteous judgment, a compound of righteousness and judgment. The day of wrath will reveal not arbitrary anger but God's perfectly just verdict. This word guards the character of God: His condemnation of sin is the fair and holy act of a righteous Judge, and it vindicates rather than tarnishes His name.

syneidesis.gloss: Conscience, the inner witness that knows with itself and testifies to right and wrong. Even Gentiles who never received the written law carry its work on their hearts, and their conscience either accuses or defends them. This inner court proves that all people have moral knowledge and so are without excuse before God.

basic.context: In chapter 1 Paul described humanity in open rebellion, suppressing the truth and given over to sin. A reader could easily agree and feel superior: those people deserve judgment. Chapter 2 turns to that reader. The one who condemns others, the moralist, the religious person who knows better, is caught in the same net, because he does the same things while claiming the moral high ground. Paul is closing every escape route so that grace can be received.
basic.hermeneutics: Read this as a courtroom scene. Paul uses a common ancient style called diatribe, arguing with an imagined objector, so the you here is a representative moralist, and by chapter 2 verse 17 clearly the Jew who trusts in the law. The point is not that anyone actually earns salvation by works, but that God's standard is impartial and real: He judges by deeds, according to truth, without favoritism. This exposes the moralist as guilty.
basic.originalLanguages: The passage turns on the language of judging. To krino others is to invite God's krima, His verdict, which is based on truth. God's kindness aims at metanoia, a real turning, not presumption. The day of wrath reveals His dikaiokrisia, righteous judgment. And even those without the written law have the work of the law on their hearts, their syneidesis, their conscience, accusing or defending them.
basic.typology: The day of wrath, when God's righteous judgment is revealed, echoes the prophetic day of the LORD, now focused on Christ Jesus as the appointed Judge. The law written on Gentile hearts anticipates the new covenant promise of the law written within, though here it functions to convict rather than to save.
basic.christ: The passage ends with the day when God judges the secrets of all through Christ Jesus. The Judge of all the earth is Jesus. This warning drives us to Him not only as Judge but as the only Savior, for if God repays each according to his deeds, and none of us can stand, then our one hope is the righteousness that comes as a gift in Christ, unfolded from chapter 3 onward.
basic.god: God is impartial, patient, and righteous. He shows no favoritism; He judges by truth and by deeds, not by ancestry or profession. His kindness is real and is meant to lead to repentance, yet He will by no means clear the guilty who presume upon it. This is a God whose judgment is trustworthy precisely because it is just.
basic.crossReferences: Psalm 62:12 and Proverbs 24:12 declare that God repays each according to his work. Matthew 7:1-2 warns that with the judgment you pronounce you will be judged. 2 Chronicles 19:7 and Acts 10:34 affirm that God shows no partiality. Jeremiah 31:33 promises the law written on the heart, fulfilled in the new covenant.
basic.oneStory: From the beginning God has been the righteous Judge of all the earth. Here Paul shows that no one, not the pagan of chapter 1 nor the moralist of chapter 2, can stand before that judgment on the basis of their deeds. This shuts every mouth and prepares the way for the good news that a righteousness from God is coming, apart from works, for all who believe.

context.literary: Romans 2:1-16 is the hinge that turns the argument of 1:18-3:20 from the openly wicked to the respectable moralist. Paul writes in diatribe style, addressing an imagined objector directly. The section is framed by the theme of judgment: it opens with the human judge who has no excuse and closes with the divine Judge who assesses the secrets of the heart through Christ.
context.historical: Paul writes around AD 57 to a mixed church of Jews and Gentiles in Rome. Many Jewish believers, and morally serious Gentiles, could distance themselves from the vices of chapter 1. Paul anticipates that self-confidence and dismantles it, insisting that possessing the law or moral standards without keeping them brings greater accountability, not exemption.
context.canonical: This passage stands with the whole biblical witness that God judges impartially and according to deeds. It does not contradict justification by faith; it establishes the need for it. By showing that judgment is by works and that no one's works can pass, Paul makes the free gift of righteousness in chapter 3 the only refuge for Jew and Gentile alike.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: Paul intends to strip away the false security of the moralist and the religiously privileged, showing that God's judgment is impartial and according to truth, so that every reader is brought under the same verdict and prepared to receive righteousness as a gift rather than a wage.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage describes the principle of God's righteous judgment, that He repays according to deeds. It prescribes for every age a warning against hypocrisy and presumption, and a call to receive God's kindness as a summons to repentance rather than a license to sin.

debates.question: Does verse 13, the doers of the law will be declared righteous, teach that some are justified by their works?

views.label: A statement of the principle, not the outcome
views.case: The historic Protestant reading holds that Paul states the true principle of judgment by works to show that no fallen person meets it. Read with 3:20, no one is justified by works of the law, this verse convicts rather than offers a second path to acceptance.

views.label: A description of the justified who then obey
views.case: Others take it to describe those already justified by faith who, transformed by the Spirit, become genuine doers of the law, so that their obedience is the evidence, not the ground, of their standing.

debates.bearing: Either way, no one is justified by law keeping apart from grace. The verse either exposes universal failure or describes the fruit of faith, and both readings drive the reader to the gift of righteousness in Christ.

debates.question: Who are the Gentiles who do by nature what the law requires in verses 14 and 15?

views.label: Pagans with the moral law on the conscience
views.case: The traditional reading sees ordinary Gentiles who, apart from Moses, still possess an innate moral knowledge that accuses or defends them, proving all people are morally accountable and without excuse.

views.label: Gentile believers with the law written within
views.case: Some argue these are Christian Gentiles in whom the new covenant promise of the law written on the heart is fulfilled by the Spirit, showing genuine obedience that shames the unbelieving Jew.

debates.bearing: On the first view Paul universalizes accountability so that none escape; on the second he anticipates the transformed obedience of faith. In context the immediate aim is clear: to leave every person, with or without the written law, without excuse.

study.originalLanguages: The heart of the passage is the language of judgment. To krino, to pass judgment on another, is to invoke a standard that condemns the judge, for God's krima, His verdict, is based on truth and cannot be dodged. God's kindness is designed to lead to metanoia, a decisive turning from sin, yet a hard heart stores up wrath for the day when His dikaiokrisia, His righteous judgment, is revealed. Even Gentiles without the written law carry its work on their hearts, and their syneidesis, their conscience, stands as an inner witness that accuses or defends, leaving no one an excuse.

typology.fulfillment: The prophetic day of wrath and judgment is fulfilled in the day when God judges the secrets of all through Christ Jesus, the appointed Judge of the living and the dead.

typology.type: The law written on the heart
typology.fulfillment: The work of the law inscribed on the Gentile conscience anticipates the new covenant promise of Jeremiah 31, the law written within, though here it serves to convict rather than to save.
The Judge of all the earth who does what is right, from Genesis 18, is revealed as impartial and true, executing His verdict through the risen Christ.

study.christ: The section climbs to a single name: God will judge the secrets of all through Christ Jesus. Jesus is the Judge before whom every hidden thing is laid bare. This is both a solemn warning and, rightly heard, a mercy, for the Judge is also the Savior. If God repays each according to his deeds, and none of us can pass that examination, then our only hope is that the Judge became the sin-bearer. The impartial justice of God, which condemns every self-righteous heart, is answered only in the crucified and risen Christ, whose gift of righteousness the next chapter will unfold.
study.god: This passage is a portrait of God as the perfectly righteous Judge. He is impartial, showing no favoritism to Jew or Gentile, religious or irreligious. He judges by truth and by deeds, not by profession or pedigree. His kindness, tolerance, and patience are genuine riches, meant to lead to repentance, yet they are not weakness; to despise them is to store up wrath. Here is a God whose holiness is unbending and whose judgment is trustworthy because it is entirely just.

crossReferences.note: You, O Lord, will repay each one according to his deeds, the text Paul cites for God's impartial recompense.
With the measure you use, it will be measured to you; Jesus warns against the self-condemning judgment Paul exposes.
God shows no favoritism but accepts those from every nation who fear Him, echoing Paul's impartial Judge.
I will put My law in their minds and write it on their hearts, the new covenant promise behind the law written within.

study.oneStory: The whole Bible presents God as the righteous Judge of all the earth. In Eden sin entered, and ever since, humanity has stood accountable. Paul here removes the last hiding place: neither the pagan nor the moralist can survive judgment by deeds, for God is impartial and sees the secrets of the heart. This universal guilt is not the end of the story but its turning point, for it makes the coming announcement of a righteousness from God, given freely in Christ, the only good news that can save.

quiz.q: Why does the person who passes judgment on others have no excuse?

options.text: Because he does the same things he condemns, so his own verdict falls on himself.
options.note: Paul's point exactly: the judge condemns himself because he practices what he judges.

options.text: Because judging others is always forbidden under any circumstances.
options.note: Close, but Paul's issue is hypocrisy in judging, not that all moral discernment is wrong.

options.text: Because only God is allowed to have opinions about right and wrong.
options.note: God alone is the final Judge, but Paul's concern here is the hypocrite who does the same things.

options.text: Because the moralist has never sinned and so cannot relate.
options.note: The opposite is true; he is guilty of the very sins he condemns.

quiz.q: What is God's kindness, tolerance, and patience meant to lead a person to?

options.text: Repentance, a real turning from sin to God.
options.note: Verse 4 says plainly that God's kindness leads you to repentance.

options.text: A softened, grateful heart that begins to hate its sin.
options.note: A right response, and part of repentance, though Paul names the turning itself as the goal.

options.text: Confidence that God will overlook sin because He is patient.
options.note: That is presumption; Paul warns it stores up wrath rather than securing pardon.

options.text: Permission to keep sinning since judgment is delayed.
options.note: This despises the riches of His kindness and hardens the heart.

quiz.q: On what basis will God judge, according to this passage?

options.text: Impartially, according to truth and according to each person's deeds.
options.note: God repays each according to his deeds and shows no favoritism.

options.text: According to a person's actual conduct, weighed without partiality.
options.note: Right in substance; the fuller answer names both truth and impartiality.

options.text: According to whether a person possessed the written law of Moses.
options.note: Having the law is not the standard; doing it is, and both Jew and Gentile are judged justly.

options.text: According to a person's ancestry and religious standing.
options.note: God shows no favoritism; pedigree and profession give no exemption.

quiz.q: What does Paul say about the Gentiles who do not have the written law?

options.text: The work of the law is written on their hearts, and their conscience accuses or defends them.
options.note: Verses 14 and 15 exactly: they show the law's work within, with conscience bearing witness.

options.text: They carry an inner moral witness that leaves them accountable to God.
options.note: True and well stated, though it omits the specific mention of conscience accusing or defending.

options.text: They are automatically innocent because they never received the law.
options.note: The reverse; the law on the heart means they too are without excuse.

options.text: They have no moral knowledge of any kind.
options.note: Paul says the opposite: the work of the law is written on their hearts.

quiz.q: Through whom will God judge the secrets of all people?

options.text: Through Christ Jesus, as proclaimed by Paul's gospel.
options.note: Verse 16 names Christ Jesus as the one through whom God judges the secrets of all.

options.text: Through the risen Lord Jesus, appointed as Judge of all.
options.note: Correct in substance; the text specifically ties this to the gospel Paul preaches.

options.text: Through the law of Moses applied to every nation.
options.note: The law exposes sin, but the Judge on that day is Christ, not the law itself.

options.text: Through each person's own conscience acting alone.
options.note: Conscience bears witness, but the Judge is Christ Jesus, not the conscience.

questions: Paul says the one who judges others often does the same things. Where are you quickest to condemn in others a fault you tolerate in yourself?
God's kindness, tolerance, and patience are meant to lead to repentance. Have you been reading God's patience as approval, and how might you respond to His kindness as the summons it really is?

God judges impartially, according to deeds and according to truth. How does the certainty of a perfectly just Judge unsettle any confidence you place in your background, reputation, or religious record?
Conscience accuses or defends even those without the written law. When your conscience accuses you, do you silence it or bring it to Christ?
God will judge the secrets of all through Christ Jesus. How does knowing that your hidden life is fully seen, yet that the Judge is also the Savior, change the way you live this week?

translationNotes.note: God will repay each one according to his deeds quotes Psalm 62:12 and echoes Proverbs 24:12. Paul states the principle of judgment by works to establish accountability, not to teach a way of earning salvation, as 3:20 makes clear.

The doers of the law who will be declared righteous states the true standard of judgment. In the flow of the argument it exposes that no fallen person meets it, since 3:20 concludes that by works of the law no one will be justified.
Some manuscripts read through Jesus Christ. The verse rounds off the section by naming Christ as the one through whom God judges the secrets of all, tying final judgment to the gospel Paul preaches.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Romans 2:1-16. Footnote markers and the Psalm 62:12 citation note were omitted from the running text per format. No em or en dashes are present in this passage's BSB text. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
